title: Air India's Widebody Bet: Integrating Vistara and Renewing the Fleet
dek: With the Vistara merger complete and a record aircraft order on the books, Air India is rebuilding its long-haul fleet and product as it pushes to reclaim a bigger share of India's international traffic.
body:
Air India enters the close of 2025 in the middle of one of the most ambitious transformations in Asian aviation. Since being acquired by the Tata Group, the carrier has absorbed Vistara into its mainline operation, placed one of the largest aircraft orders ever announced, and begun a sweeping retrofit of an ageing widebody fleet. The strategy centres on a simple ambition: capturing more of the long-haul demand that currently flows through Gulf and Southeast Asian hubs rather than nonstop from Indian cities.

Folding Vistara into the mainline

The integration of Vistara, previously a joint venture with Singapore Airlines, consolidated the Tata Group's airline interests under fewer brands and gave Air India additional aircraft, crews and slots. Singapore Airlines holds a minority stake in the enlarged carrier, an arrangement that aligns a major Star Alliance member with India's expanding outbound market.

Merging two operations with different fleets, systems and service cultures is rarely smooth, and the carrier has acknowledged that aligning cabins, scheduling and loyalty programmes is a multi-year effort.

Rebuilding the long-haul product

Air India's order book spans Airbus and Boeing widebodies and narrowbodies, positioning it to renew nearly every part of its network over the coming years. In the meantime, the airline is retrofitting existing Boeing 777 and 787 aircraft with new seats and cabins to close the gap with rivals while fresh deliveries arrive.

The competitive logic is clear. Carriers such as Emirates, Qatar Airways and Singapore Airlines have long carried Indian passengers over their hubs to Europe and North America. By expanding nonstop widebody flying from Delhi, Mumbai and Bengaluru, Air India aims to keep more of that traffic, and its yield, within India. Execution risk remains high given the scale of the fleet ramp, but the direction of the strategy is unmistakable.
heroImageAlt: A widebody twin-aisle jet in revised livery climbing after takeoff from an Indian airport
metaTitle: Air India's Widebody Strategy After the Vistara Merger
metaDescription: Air India is integrating Vistara, retrofitting its 777 and 787 fleet, and placing record aircraft orders to win back long-haul traffic from Gulf and Asian hubs.
metaKeywords: Air India,Vistara merger,Tata Group airlines,widebody fleet,Air India long-haul,Indian aviation
